title: Delete the Middle Node of a Linked List
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: C proposal for this challenge: the minimum-memory champion. The recommended and fastest-runtime proposal is the C++ one-pass solution; see crossReferences.

variants.languageReason: C has the smallest practical LeetCode runtime/stdlib baseline among the allowed languages for this linked-list deletion task. The algorithm itself allocates nothing and stores only three node pointers.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Used for the recommended and runtime proposal; equally optimal algorithmically, but its class/STL runner baseline can report more memory than C.
Selected for the memory objective: direct struct pointers, no STL, no heap allocation, no recursion, and minimal language baseline.
Considered for native performance and memory safety; ownership ceremony around LeetCode list nodes does not lower memory below C.
Considered for compiled pointer traversal; runtime and GC baseline are weaker for the low-MB target.
Considered for JIT performance; managed runtime and object-layout overhead are not competitive for the memory objective.
Considered only for simplicity; interpreter/VM memory baselines are too high for the minimum-memory goal.

variants.approachJustification: Use fast/slow pointers to place slow at the middle, retain the predecessor, and unlink the middle node without allocating any auxiliary storage.

reasoningSummary: The memory proposal uses the same optimal fast/slow predecessor-tracking algorithm as the C++ runtime proposal, but submits it in C to minimize reported language/runtime memory baseline.
